result: Each night the streetlights come on in a staggered heartbeat, one by one, like shy fireflies learning to be brave. I sit on the curb between shadow and sodium glow, boots balanced on a crack filled with cigarette ghosts, and I listen. The neighborhood is singing in frequencies only dogs and melancholic humans can register: the sigh of water cooling in radiator pipes, the wet slap of a dish towel against chrome, a skateboard two blocks away practicing its lonely percussion. Someone is making love very quietly two floors up; their sighs vibrate in the hollow of my ribcage the way thunder remembers lightning. Across the street, Mrs. Kovalenko trims brown edges from geraniums the color of late Soviet sunsets, scissors flashing like mirrored confessionals. The smell of fried onions drifts out of somebody’s kitchen window and entwines with colder air, sweeter than perfume, homelier than any kiss I’ve ever received. I think about cities as anthologies of unfinished letters; every illuminated rectangle is a paragraph that never got mailed. I think about the way asphalt holds heat like grief, and how at 3 a.m. even garbage trucks sound nostalgic. When I finally stand, my knees make small obedient sounds, approving the continuation. I pocket a pebble shaped like an infant tooth and name it Maybe. I walk home whistling, making sure to pass beneath every lamp so that my shadow has the chance to be reborn again and again, and again, slightly different each time.
